EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across the area and brough heavy snow to portions of western Wyoming and strong wind to southern and central Wyoming. The heaviest snow well in the Tetons where 22 inches of new snow fell at the Jackson Hole Ski Resort. In areas East of the Divide, high wind was main concern. Wind gusts over 70 mph occurred in Natrona County with a maximum wind gust of 78 mph along Wyoming Boulevard. In Sweetwater County, a snow squall moved west to east across the country, bringing wind gusts up to 66 mph and visibility under one quarter mile at times, disrupting traffic along Interstate 80.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell though much of the Tetons. The highest amount reported was 22 inches the top of Jackson Hole Ski Resort.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific system moved into western Wyoming and brought another round of heavy snow to western Wyoming. The heaviest snow fell in southwestern Wyoming with up to 20 inches of new snow in the Salt and Wyoming Range. Several locations in the Tetons also received over a foot of snow. Amounts in the western valleys were generally 7 to 13 inches in the Star Valley and 6 to 9 inches in the Jackson Valley. The snow combined with gusty wind that brought difficult travel and some road closures. Some areas East of the Divide had high winds, with gusts as high as 75 mph around Buffalo.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over a foot of new snow fell through most of the Salt and Wyoming Range, with a maximum of 20 inches to the east of Smoot.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful Arctic front dropped across Wyoming and brought heavy snow to portions of western Wyoming. The heaviest snow fell in the Tetons where up to 17 inches of new snow fell. Over a foot of snow fell in portions of the Salt and Wyoming Range. Amounts in the Jackson and Star Valleys generally ranged from 4 to 10 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy snow fell in the Jackson Valley, with 9 inches of new snow falling near Wilson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful Arctic front dropped across Wyoming and brought heavy snow to portions of western Wyoming. The heaviest snow fell in the Tetons where up to 17 inches of new snow fell. Over a foot of snow fell in portions of the Salt and Wyoming Range. Amounts in the Jackson and Star Valleys generally ranged from 4 to 10 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around the Star Valley, 5 to 10 inches of new snow fell. The maximum amount of 10 inches fell near Turnerville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific system moved into western Wyoming and brought another round of heavy snow to western Wyoming. The heaviest snow fell in southwestern Wyoming with up to 20 inches of new snow in the Salt and Wyoming Range. Several locations in the Tetons also received over a foot of snow. Amounts in the western valleys were generally 7 to 13 inches in the Star Valley and 6 to 9 inches in the Jackson Valley. The snow combined with gusty wind that brought difficult travel and some road closures. Some areas East of the Divide had high winds, with gusts as high as 75 mph around Buffalo.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combination of snow and strong wind brought the closure of Highway 28 over South Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across the area and brough heavy snow to portions of western Wyoming and strong wind to southern and central Wyoming. The heaviest snow well in the Tetons where 22 inches of new snow fell at the Jackson Hole Ski Resort. In areas East of the Divide, high wind was main concern. Wind gusts over 70 mph occurred in Natrona County with a maximum wind gust of 78 mph along Wyoming Boulevard. In Sweetwater County, a snow squall moved west to east across the country, bringing wind gusts up to 66 mph and visibility under one quarter mile at times, disrupting traffic along Interstate 80.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snow squall brought visibility of under a quarter mile to Eastern Sweetwater County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic air mass settled over the region for several days, producing extreme and dangerous wind chills across northeast Wyoming. Temperatures remained below zero for 3 days, as low as 20 below to 35 below zero across much of the area on the mornings of the 13th and 14th. Gusty north to northwest winds at times helped to produce wind chills in the 30 below to 65 below range from the morning of the 13th to the morning of the 15th.

Heavy snow fell across our western mountains and along the Interstate 80 Corridor from Cheyenne to Arlington on 14-15 January.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Interstate 80 closed due to winter weather between Laramie and Rawlins for an extended period of time from 8PM on the 14th through 9AM on the 15th. CoCoRaHS observer near Elk Mountain reported a 48 hour snowfall report of 11 to 13 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell across our western mountains and along the Interstate 80 Corridor from Cheyenne to Arlington on 14-15 January.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report from Facebook northeast of Laramie reported 7-8 inches of snow. Most other reports ranged from 3 to 5 inches in Laramie.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very unstable northwest flow aloft resulted in heavy snow to the Northeast Bighorn Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around a foot of snow occurred across the Northeast Bighorn Mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short period of light to moderate snowfall occurred across portions of southeast Wyoming from the afternoon of 1/7 into the morning hours on 1/8, as a cold front pushed to the south across the region. The heaviest accumulations occurred along the north-facing slopes of the northern Laramie Range in southwestern Converse County, courtesy of northerly upslope flow in the wake of the cold front. Minor travel impacts were observed along Interstate 25 west of Douglas, and along Interstate 80 near Arlington and Elk Mountain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 6 to 10 inches of snow fell along the northern slopes of the northern Laramie Range in far southwestern Converse County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds returned to the wind prone areas of southeast Wyoming the evening of 17 January and continued into 18 January.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter located 12 miles south southeast of Horse Creek reported this peak wind gust. The Otto WYDOT site, 16 miles west southwest of Cheyenne and UPR site Speer, 10 miles southwest of Cheyenne also reported wind gusts in excess of 58 mph during this timeframe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fetch of Pacific moisture moved into Wyoming and brought locally heavy snow to the Wind River Range. A foot of new snow was recorded at SNOTEL sites near South Pass City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Deer Park SNOTEL site recorded 12 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds across Carbon County in Wyoming gusting around 60 mph occurred starting early morning on the first, continuing periodically into the early afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple WYDOT mesonet stations along Interstate 80 between Rawlins and Laramie reported high winds gusting around 60 mph. WYDOT mesonet station Arlington East (WY23) reported maximum wind gust 2 miles east-southeast of Arlington at 12:52 AM MST on 3/1.

CZ_NAME: CENTRAL LARAMIE RANGE AND SOUTHWEST PLATTE COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large, slow moving area of low pressure resulted in a prolonged period of snow across much of southeast Wyoming. The mountains of southeast Wyoming saw the most notable accumulations, with 2 to 4 feet of snow being reported in the Snowy Range. Several inches of snow also fell over the lower elevations, but overall impacts were minimal due to the long duration of the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The NOHRSC National Snowfall Analysis suggests 3 to 6 inches of snow fell across the Central Laramie Range over a 48-hour period.

Peak snow amount reported was 14.8 inches at Little Snake SNOTEL, 12 miles southwest of Encampment.

Whitaker WYDOT site, 16 miles east of Horse Creek and Wyo Hill WYDOT site, 10 miles southwest of Cheyenne also reported wind gusts in excess of 58 mph during this time along Interstate 25.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving trough combined with a potent atmospheric river to bring heavy snow to western Wyoming. The highest amounts fell in the Tetons where amounts over 2 feet were common. The highest amount was 43 inches at the Jackson Hole Ski Resort. Amounts over 18 inches also fell in Yellowstone Park, the Salt and Wyoming Range and the Wind River Range. In the lower elevations, the highest snow totals were in the Jackson Valley where amounts of 1 to 2 feet fell. High wind occurred in the wind prone areas ahead of a frontal passage, with wind gusts around 60 mph in the lower elevations of Sweetwater and Natrona Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell though much of Yellowstone Park. The highest amount was 31 inches in the southwestern mountains of the park. Amounts were generally 12 to 18 inches in northern portions of the park.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were numerous reports of heavy, with amounts ranging from 12 to 20 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Around 12 PM MST, received reports of a grass fire west of Cheyenne that was rapidly spreading with strong southwest winds off the South Laramie Range gusting 30 to 40 MPH. Approximately 6,619 acres were burned 5 miles west of Cheyenne with multiple structures damaged or destroyed. Two evacuation orders were given by local officials including the Happy Valley subdivision. No total loss of primary structures were reported, but numerous outbuildings were damaged or destroyed with one completely loss garage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure passing south of Wyoming brought heavy portions to portions of central and southern Wyoming. Two areas saw the heaviest snow. The first was in the favored northerly upslope areas from the Wind River Range through Casper Mountain. The heaviest snow fell in the Wind River Range where as much as 22 inches of new snow fell. In the lower elevations, Lander picked up from 10 to 20 inches of snow. A band of snow also set up across Sweetwater County, where 6 to 14 inches of snow fell from Green River to Wamsutter. The heavy snow led to closure of Interstate 80.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell through much of the eastern slopes of the Wind River Range. The highest snowfall total was 22 inches at the Hobbs Park SNOTEL site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific trough moved across the area. This combined with a jet streak to produce locally heavy snow around Cody where 5 to 8 inches of new snow fell.

A large, slow moving area of low pressure resulted in a prolonged period of snow across much of southeast Wyoming. The mountains of southeast Wyoming saw the most notable accumulations, with 2 to 4 feet of snow being reported in the Snowy Range. Several inches of snow also fell over the lower elevations, but overall impacts were minimal due to the long duration of the event.

The Evanston-Uinta County Airport - Burns Field ASOS recorded a prolonged period of wind gusts in excess of 30 mph and visibility at 1 mile or less, coincident with an  organized band of snow that moved through the area. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter system brought strong winds and snow to areas across southwest Wyoming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm impacted the area and resulted in the closure of I-80 from Evanston, WY to Lyman, WY from approximately 11:30 am MST 1/12 to 6:30 am MST 1/13. Strong winds were also observed with this storm. A wind gust of 61 mph was recorded by the Evanston-Uinta County Airport - Burns Field ASOS, and a peak gust of 64 mph was recorded by the I80/US189 - First Divide mesonet station. Other mesonet stations in the area showed frequent gusts at or above 50, and periods of sustained winds in excess of 40 mph.

Sweetwater County recorded the coldest temperatures of 36 below at Wamsutter. The low temperatures West of the Divide fell to as low as minus 31 at Farson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On January 14, 2024, at approximately 11:30 AM MST, a skier unintentionally triggered an avalanche on a mid-elevation (8,500���), northwest-facing, sparsely treed slope above an indistinct gully on the Northwest Face of Little Poudre Mountain in Lincoln County Wyoming. The skier was killed in the avalanche. No other individuals were injured or killed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: From Bridger-Teton National Forecast Avalanche Forecast Staff: On January 14, 2024, at approximately 11:30 AM, a skier unintentionally triggered an avalanche on a mid-elevation (8,500���), northwest-facing, sparsely treed slope above an indistinct gully on the Northwest Face of Little Poudre Mountain. This large, soft slab avalanche filled a relatively narrow path. The weak snow layer formed during a prolonged dry period in December that was buried by approximately 30��� of snow falling during a series of storms that began on January 4th. The avalanche broke approximately 50��� wide and 1-3��� deep and entrained more snow along the flanks of a shallow gully. The avalanche fell approximately 600 vertical feet. The slide path is one of several sparsely wooded avalanche paths on the northwest face of Little Poudre Mountain that run the full 1,200��� length of the face. This avalanche was one of the dozens reported in the region during a particularly active avalanche cycle. The skier was killed in the avalanche. No other individuals were injured or killed. Please see avalanche.org for the full event report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful Arctic front dropped across Wyoming and brought heavy snow to portions of western Wyoming. The heaviest snow fell in the Tetons where up to 17 inches of new snow fell. Over a foot of snow fell in portions of the Salt and Wyoming Range. Amounts in the Jackson and Star Valleys generally ranged from 4 to 10 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 15 inches of new snow fell in the higher elevations of the Salt and Wyoming Range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large, organized area of low pressure tracked from the southern Plains into the Upper Midwest during the period from 1/8 to 1/10, contributing to a persistent and intense surface pressure gradient across southeast Wyoming. This resulted in an extended period of strong winds over southeast Wyoming, with the strongest winds generally observed along and west of Interstate 25. The peak wind gust for the event was 79 MPH at Strouss Hill along Interstate 80 near Arlington, with a peak gust to 67 MPH and 65 MPH at the Cheyenne Airport on 1/9 and 1/10, respectively. In the North Snowy Range Foothills near Arlington and Elk Mountain, the combination of strong winds and fresh snow pack resulted in a 3+ hour period of ground blizzard conditions along Interstate 80 between Laramie and Rawlins with visibilities under 1/8 of a mile from late in the evening on 1/8 into the early morning hours of 1/9.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Frequent wind gusts to 60+ MPH were observed at multiple sensors along I-80. A maximum wind gust to 69 MPH was observed at Otto Road (16 miles WSW of Cheyenne) at 1:20 PM MST on 1/9.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of strong wind gusts occurred across the southeast Wyoming wind corridors from the 11th through the 12th, in advance of an intense arctic cold front which swept across the region later in the day on the 12th. Peak wind gusts reached 79 MPH near Arlington along Interstate 80 between Laramie and Rawlins. Minor snowfall accumulations of 3 to 6 inches also occurred along the Cheyenne and Pine Ridges in southern Goshen and eastern Niobrara county as a result of a weak initial frontal passage between the 10th and 11th. In the wake of the intense arctic cold front that followed, temperatures plunged to between -15 F and -30 F across much of southeast Wyoming, with localized areas of temperatures approaching -40 F. This combined with elevated wind speeds resulted in a prolonged period of wind chills of -35 F to -55 F across a large portion of southeast Wyoming from the evening of 1/12 through the morning of 1/16. Daytime high temperatures ranged from -5 F to -15 F for many locations on 1/13, with Cheyenne and Laramie experiencing their coldest high temperature on record for that date.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 2 to 4 inches of snow fell across Niobrara County from the mid to late evening hours on 1/10 through the afternoon on 1/11, per multiple data sources. CoCoRaHS observer in Lusk reported 3.5 inches of snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell across our western mountains and along the Interstate 80 Corridor from Cheyenne to Arlington on 14-15 January.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report received 11.1 miles northwest of Cheyenne in the South Laramie Range Foothills of 6.5 inches. Public called to report 8 inches south of Cheyenne near Terry Bison Ranch Road and US 85.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A second wave of extreme cold occurred across the North Snowy Range Foothills from 1/15 through 1/16, with minimum observed air temperatures ranging from -10 F to -20 F. The cold temperatures combined with gusty winds resulted in a prolonged period of extreme wind chills under -35 F. Minimum observed wind chill values reached -47 F at the Dana Ridge WYDOT sensor (11 miles SSW of Hanna) at 10:10 PM MST on 1/15.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overnight low temperatures fell to between -15 F and -25 F across Goshen County from 1/12 into 1/13, with daytime highs generally remaining below -5 F during the afternoon on 1/13. The combination of cold temperatures and gusty winds resulted in a prolonged period of extreme wind chills under -30 F. Minimum observed wind chill values reached -50 F at the BRAUN1 UPR sensor (12 miles S of Van Tassel) at 4:24 AM MST on 1/13.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy snow was reported across portions of southeast Wyoming as a low-pressure system moved across central Wyoming and eastern Colorado. This resulted in extended closures to Interstate 80 between Cheyenne and Laramie upwards of 10 inches of snow fell along the South Laramie Range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous CoCoRaHS stations around the town of Laramie reported between 5 and 7 inches of snowfall. Additionally, an emergency manager reported 7.6 inches of snowfall 9 miles east-northeast of Woods Landing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure passing south of Wyoming brought heavy portions to portions of central and southern Wyoming. Two areas saw the heaviest snow. The first was in the favored northerly upslope areas from the Wind River Range through Casper Mountain. The heaviest snow fell in the Wind River Range where as much as 22 inches of new snow fell. In the lower elevations, Lander picked up from 10 to 20 inches of snow. A band of snow also set up across Sweetwater County, where 6 to 14 inches of snow fell from Green River to Wamsutter. The heavy snow led to closure of Interstate 80.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 10 to 20 inches, with the higher amounts toward Sinks Canyon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of strong wind gusts occurred across the southeast Wyoming wind corridors from the 11th through the 12th, in advance of an intense arctic cold front which swept across the region later in the day on the 12th. In the wake of the intense arctic cold front that followed, temperatures plunged to between -15 F and -30 F across much of southeast Wyoming, with localized areas of temperatures approaching -40 F. Daytime high temperatures ranged from -5 F to -15 F for many locations on 1/13, with Cheyenne and Laramie experiencing their coldest high temperature on record for that date.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CoCoRaHS observer reported 3.5 inches of snow 3 miles NNE of Hawk Springs in southern Goshen county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large, organized area of low pressure tracked from the southern Plains into the Upper Midwest during the period from 1/8 to 1/10, contributing to a persistent and intense surface pressure gradient across southeast Wyoming. This resulted in an extended period of strong winds over southeast Wyoming, with the strongest winds generally observed along and west of Interstate 25. The peak wind gust for the event was 79 MPH at Strouss Hill along Interstate 80 near Arlington, with a peak gust to 67 MPH and 65 MPH at the Cheyenne Airport on 1/9 and 1/10, respectively. In the North Snowy Range Foothills near Arlington and Elk Mountain, the combination of strong winds and fresh snow pack resulted in a 3+ hour period of ground blizzard conditions along Interstate 80 between Laramie and Rawlins with visibilities under 1/8 of a mile from late in the evening on 1/8 into the early morning hours of 1/9.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A maximum wind gust of 62 MPH was observed at the Banner Draw (PC382) Mesonet site (11 miles SW of Glenrock) at 1:50 PM MST on 1/9.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave moved across Wyoming and brought severe thunderstorms to portions of central Wyoming. A 60 mph wind gust was reported at the Lander airport in Fremont County. In Johnson County, quarter size hail was reported to the southeast of Linch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported hail up to quarter size that accumulated 1 to 2 inches on the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing cold front and shortwave combined with a moisture moving in from around an upper level low to produce showers and thunderstorms, some with strong wind gusts. The storms mainly hit Fremont and Natrona Counties. One round of showers and storms hit around noon, with a wind gust of 58 mph reported along Hat Six Road to the East of Evansville. The second round of storms occurred in the evening hours, producing wind gusts over 58 mph from South Pass in the Wind River Mountains to the Casper Airport. Some notable wind gusts included 66 mph over South Pass and 63 mph at Natrona.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of strong wind gusts occurred across the southeast Wyoming wind corridors from the 11th through the 12th, in advance of an intense arctic cold front which swept across the region later in the day on the 12th. Peak wind gusts reached 79 MPH near Arlington along Interstate 80 between Laramie and Rawlins. Minor snowfall accumulations of 3 to 6 inches also occurred along the Cheyenne and Pine Ridges in southern Goshen and eastern Niobrara county as a result of a weak initial frontal passage between the 10th and 11th. In the wake of the intense arctic cold front that followed, temperatures plunged to between -15 F and -30 F across much of southeast Wyoming, with localized areas of temperatures approaching -40 F. This combined with elevated wind speeds resulted in a prolonged period of wind chills of -35 F to -55 F across a large portion of southeast Wyoming from the evening of 1/12 through the morning of 1/16. Daytime high temperatures ranged from -5 F to -15 F for many locations on 1/13, with Cheyenne and Laramie experiencing their coldest high temperature on record for that date.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overnight low temperatures ranged from -25 F to -30 F across the northern Laramie Range from 1/12 into 1/13, with daytime highs remaining near -10 F to -15 F during the afternoon on 1/13. The combination of cold temperatures and gusty winds resulted in a prolonged period of extreme wind chills under -40 F. Minimum observed wind chill values reached -52 F at Mesonet Station PC248 (2 miles NNE of Esterbrook) at 8:00 AM MST on 1/13.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific trough and cold front combined with an atmospheric river to bring heavy snow to much of western Wyoming and portions of central Wyoming. The heaviest snow fell Tetons where the snow was multiple feet fell. The highest amount was 29 inches at the top of the Jackson Hole Ski Resort. Other notable amounts in western Wyoming included 22 inches at Pahaska, 19 inches at Blind Bull Summit in the Salt and Wyoming Range and 20 inches at the Deer Park SNOTEL site in the Wind River Range. Heavy snow fell in the western valleys as well with 1 to 2 feet through the Jackson Valley and 8 to 15 inches the Star Valley. In Hot Springs County, snowbanding developed and brought 8 inches of snow near Lucerne.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over a foot of snow fell across portions of the Absarokas, with a maximum of 21 inches at Pahaska.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific trough moved across western and central Wyoming and brought heavy snow to northwestern Wyoming and high wind to much of the area. The heaviest snow fell in the Tetons where a maximum of 24 inches of new snow fell at the Grand Targhee Ski Resort. A foot of new snow fell at Old Faithful in Yellowstone Park. High wind was much more widespread. Wind gusts as high as 99 mph occurred along the Chief Joseph Highway in Park County. At Red Canyon along South Pass, a combination of snow and wind gusts as high as 97 mph led to the closure of Highway 28 over the pass. Wind gusts over 60 mph were also reported in the lower elevations of Park, Hot Springs, Fremont, Natrona and Sweetwater Counties. At the Natrona County airport, strong crosswinds flipped a small plane over. The occupants were not injured.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At Old Faithful, 12 inches of new snow fell.

The heaviest snow fell in the Tetons where a maximum of 24 inches of new snow fell at the Grand Targhee Ski Resort. A foot of new snow fell at Old Faithful in Yellowstone Park. High wind was much more widespread. Wind gusts as high as 99 mph occurred along the Chief Joseph Highway in Park County. At Red Canyon along South Pass, a combination of snow and wind gusts as high as 97 mph led to the closure of Highway 28 over the pass. Wind gusts over 60 mph were also reported in the lower elevations of Park, Hot Springs, Fremont, Natrona and Sweetwater Counties. At the Natrona County airport, strong crosswinds flipped a small plane over. The occupants were not injured.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of high wind occurred in Natrona County, with wind gusts as high as 72 mph along Wyoming Boulevard and Hat Six Roads. At the Natrona County airport, a twin engine plane was caught in strong cross winds and was flipped over. The two occupants were uninjured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific trough and cold front combined with an atmospheric river to bring heavy snow to much of western Wyoming and portions of central Wyoming. The heaviest snow fell Tetons where the snow was multiple feet fell. The highest amount was 29 inches at the top of the Jackson Hole Ski Resort. Other notable amounts in western Wyoming included 22 inches at Pahaska, 19 inches at Blind Bull Summit in the Salt and Wyoming Range and 20 inches at the Deer Park SNOTEL site in the Wind River Range. Heavy snow fell in the western valleys as well with 1 to 2 feet through the Jackson Valley and 8 to 15 inches the Star Valley. In Hot Springs County, snowbanding developed and brought 8 inches of snow near Lucerne.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in the western Wind River Range, with a maximum amount of 18 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific system moved into Wyoming and brought heavy snow to portions of the state. The storm was a fairly warm system so snow amounts were fairly elevation dependent. The Star Valley saw locally heavy snow, with a foot of new snow near Turnerville. Favorable upslope flow brought heavy snow around Lander as well, with amounts ranging from 5 to 11 inches, with the highest amounts in the foothills of the Wind River Range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong spring storm system brought heavy rain and gusty winds to much of northeast Wyoming. Across the higher elevations of northeast Wyoming, mainly above 5000 feet, temperatures were cold enough for some snow to accumulate. The highest snowfall amounts were generally between O'Neill Pass and the Four Corners area in the Black Hills and across the Bear Lodge Mountains, where 6 inches to over a foot of accumulation were reported in some areas. Some power outages were reported due to the heavy, wet snow and gusty winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough moved into Wyoming and was supportive of numerous showers and thunderstorms. The strongest storms moved through Fremont and Natrona Counties, where wind gusts of over 70 mph were reported. In Hudson, numerous trees were uprooted or snapped. In Lander, a tree was uprooted and fell on a truck. Maximum measured wind gusts included 72 mph near Lander and 70 mph on the east side of Casper. A spotter estimated a wind gust of 77 mph in Bar Nunn.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Maximum wind gusts of 72 mph were reported to the northwest of Lander.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Three thunderstorms with heavy rainfall moved over the Trapper Creek drainage in short succession during Saturday afternoon, causing localized flash flooding near the confluence of Trapper Creek and Shell Creek. Culverts were plugged and at least one resident's driveway bridge was washed out. Boulders the size of wheelbarrows were moved onto agricultural land and damaged fences. A large number of rattlesnakes and cutthroat trout were washed onto fields in the area.
Severe thunderstorms with strong wind gusts over 60 mph occurred over portions of southeast Wyoming on the 15th.

Two wildfires within close proximity of each other ignited on July 30th along the Platte-Goshen County border in southeast Wyoming. One of the fires was determined to be started by a lightning strike. Extreme wildfire conditions resulted in both of these fires merging into one fire burning 28,984 acres. This fire caused evacuations of Hartville, WY, road closures along US Highway 26 near Guernsey, WY. This fire continued into August before containment was reached on August 7th (see similar August StormData entry).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple wildfires initially along the Platte-Goshen County border northeast of Guernsey merged into the Pleasant Valley Wildfire burning 28,984 acres. Resulted in the evacuation of Hartville, WY and a multi-day closure of US Highway 26. One outbuilding was destroyed along with miles of fencing and power poles. This wildfire continued into August before being contained on August 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense supercell thunderstorm originated near Chugwater, Wyoming, during the early to mid afternoon hours on 6/20, tracking across southern Platte and Goshen counties through approximately 3:00 PM MDT before moving into the western Nebraska Panhandle. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable southwest flow aloft was over the Billings Forecast Area. Large-scale ascent ahead of a midlevel trough tracked eastward across the northern Rockies. Thunderstorms developed over the mountains of southwest Montana and moved east onto the adjacent plains. Wind shear of around 50kts supported storm organization resulting in organized thunderstorm clusters and supercells, some of which produced large hail and severe outflow winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downed power lines and tree limbs were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 59 MPH was measured by a car roof anemometer in Hawk Springs.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado was observed in rural Goshen County by numerous storm spotters|and emergency management personnel. Due to the rural nature of this tornado, only|limited damage to fencing was found, as well as scattered plant debris. Review of|video and photo sequences along with radar time-matching indicate that this tornado|likely began as a landspout, but quickly gained mesocyclonic characteristics as it|interacted with the updraft base, condensed a funnel, and slowed/occluded into the|storm. Regardless, without any damage of note this tornado will be recorded as EF-U.

The lightning-caused Elk Fire started near Riley Point and West Pass in the Bighorn National Forest. The fire was detected at 4:00 p.m. MDT on Friday, September 27th. Air attack and ground crews initially responded, which included retardant and bucket drops from airtankers and a helicopter. Hand crews and more resources arrived shortly thereafter. The Elk Fire burned in heavy, dead and down timber. The terrain is rough and remote which made it challenging for firefighters to get in or out of the incident. Shortly after the fire started, a closure for the area took place to protect the safety of firefighters and visitors traveling forest roads and trails. In addition, US Highway 14 in both directions from Burgess Junction to Dayton, Wyoming was closed for a period of time. As of this writing, 96,197 acres have burned and the fire was 48% contained. |For more detailed information, please see https://inciweb.wildfire.gov/incident-information/wybhf-elk-fire-2024.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Please see Episode Narrative.

The Fish Creek fire continued to burn across Bridger-Teton National Forest throughout the month, leading to numerous closures of US 26 over Togwotee Pass. The Pack Creek fire was started by lightning 23 miles southeast of Moran on September 15 and spread northeastward. These two fires merged toward the end of the month. By September 30th, the fires had burned over 40000 acres. Please see later Storm Data for the conclusion of these two fires.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Second part of the event for the next day. Halleck Ridge, Arlington, Arlington East, Foote Creek, and Mile Marker 249 all reported high wind gusts over 58 mph for 4 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Locally strong winds up to 88 mph were reported along Interstate 80, Interstate 25, and nearby state highways between Noon September 17th. After a brief lull, winds picked back up to High Wind Warning criteria early in the morning on September 18th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong system approached the region for the start of March, with anomalously strong low level flow noted ahead of a cold frontal boundary. This strong low level flow resulted in a prolonged period of strong sustained and gusty winds across southwest Wyoming, coinciding with accumulating snowfall along and behind the cold frontal boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Evanston-Uinta County Airport - Burns Field ASOS measured the first wind gust exceeding high wind criteria, with an initial high wind gust of 59 mph at 2:20 am MST 3/1. This ASOS also measured a peak wind gust of 66 mph at 5:26 pm MST 3/1 (and at other times as well), and a final high wind gust of 59 mph at 12:53 pm MST 3/2. While widespread warning criteria snow amounts were not reported, strong post-frontal wind gusts continued through 3/3 coinciding with periods of moderate to heavy snow with visibility below 0.25 SM at times. Additionally, derived analyses indicates a potential that warning criteria snowfall amounts occurred across portions of the north slopes of the Uinta Mountains, as well as east of SR-150 and south of I-80.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough brushed across southern Wyoming, resulting in enough support for the development of scattered high-based thunderstorms, and isolated severe level wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Evanston North mesonet station recorded a wind gust of 73 mph as a result of outflow winds associated with a nearby cluster of showers and thunderstorms moving through the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong thunderstorms developed along the Laramie Range in southeast Wyoming during the afternoon of September 15th. Segments of this line moved east into the high plains along and east of Interstate 25, and produced mainly quarter sized hail and isolated wind gusts over 60 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of quarter sized hail in Torrington Wyoming and gusty winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The lightning-caused Elk Fire started near Riley Point and West Pass in the Bighorn National Forest. The fire was detected at 4:00 p.m. MDT on Friday, September 27th. Air attack and ground crews initially responded, which included retardant and bucket drops from airtankers and a helicopter. Hand crews and more resources arrived shortly thereafter. The Elk Fire burned in heavy, dead and down timber. The terrain is rough and remote which made it challenging for firefighters to get in or out of the incident. Shortly after the fire started, a closure for the area took place to protect the safety of firefighters and visitors traveling forest roads and trails. In addition, US Highway 14 in both directions from Burgess Junction to Dayton, Wyoming was closed for a period of time. The fire burned 98,352 acres and was 100% contained by November 1st.|For more detailed information, please see https://inciweb.wildfire.gov/incident-information/wybhf-elk-fire-2024.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Please see Episode Narrative.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought widespread beneficial precipitation to most of northeast Wyoming. Temperatures became cold enough for rain to change over to snow fairly quickly during the daytime of the 29th, then persisted into the morning hours of the 30th. The heaviest snow fell during the afternoon and evening hours. Snowfall amounts were heaviest across portions of southern Campbell County and the Bear Lodge Mountains. The highest amounts were reported in the Wright and Sundance areas, 7 to 14 inches, with amounts to around 6 inches elsewhere across southern Campbell County, the Gillette and Moorcroft areas, and Crook and western Weston counties. Travel was most impacted across southern Campbell County and portions of Interstate 90.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first substantial winter storm of the season moved in from the west and brought heavy snow and difficult travel conditions to portions of central Wyoming. The heaviest snow fell in the favored northeast flow upslope locations of the Wind River Range, Lander Foothills, and southern Natrona County. The highest total snow was 14 inches on Casper Mountain, with up to a foot of snow in and around the city of Casper. Locations in and around Lander received anywhere from 6 to 12 inches of new snow. A combination of heavy snow and winds gusting over 30 mph resulted in the closure of South Pass for several hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combination of heavy snow and wind gusting to over 30 mph led to the closure of Highway 28 over South Pass for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Embers from a burn barrel on the southern bank of the Wind River near Riverton, WY  sparked a fire during the mid-afternoon hours. Gusty wind spread the fire rapidly to the northeast, with the fire jumping over the Wind River a couple hours after initial ignition. Firefighters were able to contain the fire on the south side of Riverton with minimal property damage. The fire was mostly contained by sunset. In total, the fire burned around 175 acres.

The Fish Creek / Pack Trail fires combined and we reported under the Pack Trail fire name starting on October 5. The fire continued to spread through the first half of the month. Spreading slowed somewhat when the first snow arrived on October 16th, the a more substantial snow at the end of the month led to the fire being 84 percent contained by October 31st. The fire caused more periodic closures of US 26 over Togwotee Pass through the month. By October 31st, the fire had burned more than 89,000 acres.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Please see episode for a description.

A thunderstorm became severe in northwest Crook County and tracked east-southeast across the county. The storm produced hail up to baseball size and wind gusts to 80 mph. The Devils Tower area sustained significant damage to trees and property. The storm also became tornadic for a brief period over the Bear Lodge Mountains, south of Alva. Many trees were either uprooted or snapped in half along the path, but no property was impacted by the tornado.

The House Draw fire was sparked by by a lightning strike on the afternoon of August 21. Wind gusting to 40 mph spread the fire rapidly to the north and south of the ignition point, eventually consuming around 175000 acres before being mostly contained by August 30. At the height of the fire, Interstate 90 was closed for several hours to the east of Buffalo. Although no homes were lost in the fire, damage was extensive to ranch infrastructure with around 600 miles of fence burned along with the loss of storage and water tanks, outbuildings, feed., and equipment. Losses on private land alone were estimated by an unnamed source in the Green River Star local newspaper to be 25 million dollars, not including losses to public land and highway infrastructure. Have included this number as a preliminary damage cost estimate.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado developed over the Bear Lodge Mountains, about 8 miles south of Alva, and tracked east-southeast for about two and a half miles. Numerous pine trees were either uprooted or snapped in half along its path. The estimated damage width was over a quarter mile in some areas, although some of the damage on the southern edge of the tornado was more consistent with straight line winds. No damage to property was noted. The tornado was on the ground for approximately 10 minutes with a speed of about 15 to 20 mph. The tornado was rated as an EF-1 with estimated winds up to 110 mph.

There was one injury when a person suffered an elbow contusion from flying debris at a campground and had to receive medical attention.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving thunderstorm brought heavy rain and small hail to Opal. The rain and hail overwhelmed the street sewers and resulted in flooding of 5 houses and a few streets.

Rainfall amount is unknown due to not having any know weather stations in the area. Five homes experienced flash flooding, with about 6 inches of water in basements.

Showers and thunderstorms developed off the Wasatch and Uinta Mountains mid-afternoon on Sunday the 18th of August. They crossed into southwest Sweetwater County and brought locally heavy rainfall. At around 6 PM, the river gauge on the Henry���s Fork at Manila began to spike and exceeded Flood Stage by 7:30 PM. The gauge crested near 12.5 feet at around 8:45 PM and quickly dropped back below flood stage by 10 PM. A few reports were gathered from local officials and via social media describing and showing minor flooding just north of Manila UT in the Henry's Fork river drainage. A flood survey was conducted on Aug 26 by NWS RIW and CYS employees. Several locations of agricultural flooding and significant bank erosion upstream of the gauge were noted, but no infrastructure damages were observed or known.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two wildfires within close proximity of each other ignited along the Platte-Goshen County border in southeast Wyoming. One of the fires was determined to be started by a lightning strike. Extreme wildfire conditions resulted in both of these fires merging into one fire burning 28,984 acres. This fire caused evacuations of Hartville, WY, road closures along US Highway 26 near Guernsey, WY. These fire started in July before containment was reached on August 7th (see similar July StormData entry).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple wildfires initially along the Platte-Goshen County border northeast of Guernsey merged into the Pleasant Valley Wildfire burning 28,984 acres. Resulted in the evacuation of Hartville, WY and a multi-day closure of US Highway 26. One outbuilding was destroyed along with miles of fencing and power poles. This wildfire began on July 30th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Remington Fire began in Sheridan County Wyoming and quickly spread north into Montana as a result of a low-level nocturnal jet. Total acreage burned is estimated at 196,368 acres. As of this report, the cause of the wildfire remains unknown. Total agricultural and structural damage is also unknown. Extensive air attacks were used by the firefighters during the lead-up to the formation of the low level nocturnal jet. In addition, the Northern Cheyenne Tribal Council issued a mandatory evacuation for the town of Birney, Montana. ||For more detailed information, please see inciweb.wildfire.gov/incident-information/mtgnf-remington-fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Please see Episode Narrative.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level system slowly crossed the region, bringing snowfall to the Wyoming Black Hills area. A series of disturbances within the trough brought periods of snowfall over a 2 to 3 day period. The highest snowfall amounts were over the higher elevations of the Wyoming Black Hills and Bear Lodge Mountains. Snowfall totals of 5 to 9 inches were common across the area, with localized amounts to around 12 inches. Amounts of 3 to 5 inches were noted in the Newcastle area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing cold front and upper-level shortwave brought gusty northwest winds and colder air to the area. The gusty winds and moisture in place helped to produce a prolonged period of heavy upslope snowfall during the morning and afternoon hours across parts of the Wyoming Black Hills and Bear Lodge Mountains. Amounts of 5 to 12 inches were common across the higher Wyoming Black Hills, especially the Four Corners area to O'Neill Pass. Amounts of 2 to 6 inches fell over the rest of the Wyoming Black Hills, Bear Lodge Mountains and parts of northeast Crook County. The heavy snow impacted travel across a good part of the area during the daytime and evening hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a mild and dry start to the month, a Pacific weather system brought heavy snow to portions of western Wyoming and high wind to portions of central Wyoming. The heaviest snow fell in the Tetons with the high amount of 22 inches at the Jackson Hole Ski Resort. Over a foot of snow also fell in the western slopes of the Wind River Range and the Salt and Wyoming Range. In the western valleys, up to 9 inches fell in the Jackson Valley and up to 11 inches in the Star Valley. High wind occurred in some of the favored locations East of the Divide as well. The highest wind gust reported was 86 mph at Red Canyon along South Pass. Numerous locations of the south side of Casper had wind gusts over 60 mph as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Blind Bull Summit SNOTEL site picked up 12 inches of new snow, with 12 inches falling at Commissary Ridge as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another Pacific weather system moved into western Wyoming and brought another round of moderate to heavy snow to western Wyoming. The heaviest snow fell in the Salt and Wyoming Range where 20 inches of new snow fell at the Willow Creek SNOTEL site. At the Jackson Hole Ski Resort, 16 inches of new snow fell with 12 inches at the Larson Creek SNOTEL site in the Wind River Range. In the lower elevations, 12 inches of new snow fell north of Jackson with 13 inches to the south of Alpine.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were several reports of over a foot of new snow in the Tetons, with a maximum of 16 inches at the Jackson Hole Ski Resort.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds persisted across the usual southeast Wyoming wind prone zones the afternoon of December 10th and continued through the day on December 11th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The final update on the Pack Trail was issued on November 3, 2024, with closures being lifted the following day. Winter weather and snow on and near the fire had increased confinement to 95 percent, with only isolated interior hot spots remaining. In total, the fire is estimated to have burned 89,930 acres.

A tight pressure gradient developed on November 13 as high pressure built in behind a departing cold front, bringing high wind to portions of Natrona and Park Counties. The strongest wind gust reported was 75 mph along the Chief Joseph Highway in Park County. In Natrona County, wind gusted as high as 69 mph along the wind prone areas in south Casper. A 60 mph wind gust was reported at the Casper airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts as high as 75 mph were reported along the Chief Joseph Highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific trough and cold front moved into western Wyoming and brought locally heavy snow. The heaviest snow fell in the Tetons where 15 inches of new snow fell. The heavy snow caused the closure of South Pass for a time. Over a foot of snow fell in the highest elevations of the Salt and Wyoming Range as well. A COOP Observer reported 9 inches of new snow at Jackson Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Willow Creek SNOTEL site, 13 inches of new snow fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Winter Storm Warning was issued for the Snowy Range from 2am December 17th through 8pm December 17th. A Winter Weather Advisory was issued for the Sierra Madres for the same time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Medicine Bow SNOTEL in the Snowy Range. Elevation 10512 feet. Snow weater equivalent is 1.3 inches and the average snow to liquid ratio used was 17.5 to 1. Accumulation was over 11 hours ending at 4pm December 17th. Warning critieria was reached at approximately 9am December 17th. Snow start time was 5am December 17th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level low moved across Wyoming and brought enough shear to support a landspout tornado in Sublette County, along with 2 other funnels. The tornado remained over open ground and produced no known damage. A pair of cold air funnels formed near Natrona in western Natrona County, but there was no evidence of a circulation at the ground.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sublette County emergency manager reported a landspout east of Marbleton. The landspout remained over open country and caused no known damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The season's first heavy snow occurred 25 to 27 November in the Snowy and Sierra Madre Ranges.

Other notable reports from area SNOTELs was 24.4 inches at Medicine Bow (MBSW4) and 25.1 inches at North French Creek SNOTEL (NFCW4).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong ridge of high pressure brought record high temperatures to many locations across western and central Wyoming. In addition, 4 sites had their latest occurrence of a 90 degree high temperature in history. The most notable site was Lander, which has records dating back to 1891.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Johnson County airport had a record high temperature of 94 degrees. This was the latest occurrence of 90 degrees in station history.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of September 25, the Riverton airport recorded a high temperature of 90 degrees. This was a record high temperature and also the latest occurrence of a 90 degree high temperature in station history. Four days later this latest 90 F or greater record was broken on September 29.

A line of high based showers and thunderstorms moved off of the Wind River Range and brought high wind to portions of central Wyoming. Wind gusts over 58 mph were reported from Lander to west of Riverton. Some notable wind gusts included 71 mph at the Lander Airport and 68 mph to the west of Riverton.

Strong mid level winds mixed to the surface behind a cold front and brought high wind to portions of Natrona and Park Counties. The strongest wind occurred in the wind prone areas to the south and east of Casper. A weather station along Scenic Drive registered wind gusts as high as 82 mph with other locations having wind gusts over 58 mph. Wind gusts as high as 61 mph occurred in the lee of the Absarokas near Cody.

A shortwave crossed Wyoming and brought locally severe weather. The most notable weather was a short-duration non-supercell tornado, aka landspout, which moved through downtown Thermopolis and was captured on area security cameras. Sheet metal roofing and car windows were damaged by a combination of this circulation and debris thrown by broader thunderstorm outflow, with gusts estimated between 65 to 75 mph (EF0). Meanwhile, at the Sweetwater County airport, a 59 mph wind gust was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter-like system moved through southwest Wyoming, resulting in periods of heavy snow and visibility below one quarter of a mile.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow Squall. Wyoming DOT and the public reported Interstate 80 Westbound closure near milepost 13 due to multiple crashes and a pileup as a result of a snow squall. In the hour preceding the incidents WYDOT RWIS sensors at West Divide and Painter recorded heavy snow and visibility down to between one quarter and one eighth of a mile with an accompanying flash freeze. Gusty winds did not accompany these squalls. Time is approximate.
